original_hypothesis.title: TraitForgeNft whitelist Merkle leaf encoding mismatch can lock out intended whitelisted users (case/format sensitivity)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: TraitForgeNft computes leaf as keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)), while the provided Merkle tree generator hashes addresses with keccak256(addressString) in JS. If address normalization differs (case checksum, string vs 20-byte encoding), proofs may fail for legitimate users, causing mint DoS during whitelist phase.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a common integration bug: JS libraries often hash the ASCII string representation, while Solidity hashes the 20-byte address. Unless the generator uses the 20-byte packed address bytes, the roots won't match, and whitelist minting will be unusable.
deployment_assumptions: Merkle tree leaves are generated from address strings (ASCII) rather than 20-byte packed addresses matching Solidity's abi.encodePacked(address)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: genMerkleTreeLib.ts uses `keccak256(leaf)` where `leaf` is an address string from WHITELIST; Solidity uses `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: leaf = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)) vs JS keccak256(addressString) leaf generation

preconditions: Root hash set from a tree generated using a different leaf encoding than Solidity

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: TraitForgeNft.sol onlyWhitelisted modifier; scripts/genMerkleTreeLib.ts leaf hashing
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: legitimate users cannot mint; protocol revenue/launch disrupted
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No on-chain helper to compute leaf and validate against expected format; no dual-hash support

analysis_questions: Does genMerkleTreeLib hash the 20-byte address bytes or the ASCII string?
Is WHITELIST normalized (lowercase) and consistent with Solidity encoding?
Are proofs built with tree.getProof vs getHexProof (buffer format mismatches)?
validation_requirements: Recompute leaf in JS exactly as Solidity: keccak256(solidityPacked(['address'],[addr])) and compare
End-to-end test: generate proof with scripts, call mintToken, verify passes

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: No attacker needed: a leaf mismatch causes all whitelist mints to revert with 'Not whitelisted user', effectively a launch-blocking DoS until rootHash or generator is fixed.
prerequisites: Incorrect off-chain Merkle generation or inconsistent address normalization

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: whitelist mint phase unusable; delays and lost revenue
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can update rootHash; may require regenerating proofs

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No automated test proving whitelist proofs generated by scripts pass on-chain

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: High revert rate on mintToken/mintWithBudget with 'Not whitelisted user'

validation_result.mechanism: Merkle leaf encoding mismatch (string vs packed-bytes)
validation_result.impact_summary: Whitelist Merkle proofs generated by genMerkleTreeLib.ts will never pass on-chain verification in TraitForgeNft, blocking all whitelisted minting during the whitelist window.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Solidity contract computes the Merkle leaf as keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)), which hashes the raw 20-byte address. The JavaScript generator (genMerkleTreeLib.ts) computes leaves as keccak256(addressString), where addressString is the ASCII/UTF-8 representation of the address (e.g., '0x1234...abcd', 42 bytes). The npm `keccak256` package treats string inputs as UTF-8 buffers, so it hashes 42 bytes, not 20 bytes. These two hash inputs are fundamentally different and will always produce different hashes. Consequently, the Merkle root set on-chain (derived from 42-byte-string-hashed leaves) will never validate proofs against 20-byte-packed-address leaves computed in Solidity. Every call to mintToken or mintWithBudget during the whitelist period will revert with 'Not whitelisted user'.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The on-chain leaf computation is at TraitForgeNft.sol lines 129 and 149 (modifier argument: keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))). The modifier is at lines 44-52, calling MerkleProof.verify(proof, rootHash, leaf). The off-chain leaf computation is in genMerkleTreeLib.ts line 6: `const leaves = address.map(leaf => keccak256(leaf))` where `leaf` is a string from the WHITELIST array. The deployment script (deployTasks.ts) lines 39-41 generates and sets the root hash using this mismatched generator. The encoding difference (20-byte address vs 42-byte UTF-8 string) is irreconcilable.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The leaf parameter is keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)) which hashes the 20-byte raw address. The rootHash was generated from leaves hashed as keccak256 of the 42-byte ASCII string representation. These never match.
Computes leaf as keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)), a 20-byte packed address hash. This will always differ from the off-chain string-hashed leaf, causing MerkleProof.verify to return false.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
const generateMerkleTree = (address: string[]) => {
  const leaves = address.map(leaf => keccak256(leaf));
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Hashes address strings (e.g., '0xAbCd...1234') as UTF-8 bytes (42 bytes), producing different hashes than Solidity's abi.encodePacked(address) which is 20 bytes.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls mintToken(proof) during whitelist period (block.timestamp <= whitelistEndTime)
2. onlyWhitelisted modifier triggered with leaf = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)) — hashes 20 raw address bytes
4. rootHash was set via setRootHash() in deployment (deployTasks.ts:39-41)
5. rootHash was generated by genMerkleTreeLib.ts which hashed address strings (42 UTF-8 bytes) as leaves
6. The on-chain leaf (20-byte hash) ≠ any leaf in the Merkle tree (42-byte hash)
7. MerkleProof.verify returns false, require fails with 'Not whitelisted user'
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The deployment script (deployTasks.ts lines 39-41) calls generateMerkleTree(WHITELIST) from genMerkleTreeLib.ts, which at line 6 computes leaves via `keccak256(leaf)` where `leaf` is an address string. The npm `keccak256` package hashes string input as UTF-8 bytes. An address string like '0x1234...abcd' is 42 bytes of UTF-8. The resulting Merkle root is set on-chain via `nft.setRootHash(rootHash)`. When a user calls mintToken at TraitForgeNft.sol:129, the modifier at line 136 computes `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))` which produces the keccak256 of exactly 20 bytes. Since keccak256(20_bytes) ≠ keccak256(42_UTF8_bytes) for any address, the proof verification at line 47 always fails during the whitelist window.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Merkle tree leaves are generated from address strings (ASCII) rather than 20-byte packed addresses matching Solidity's abi.encodePacked(address)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deployTasks.ts lines 39-41 show `const { rootHash } = generateMerkleTree(WHITELIST); await nft.setRootHash(rootHash);`. genMerkleTreeLib.ts line 6 computes `const leaves = address.map(leaf => keccak256(leaf))` where each leaf is a string from WHITELIST. The `keccak256` npm package hashes strings as UTF-8 buffers (42 bytes for a hex address), not as 20-byte packed addresses.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: No attacker is needed. This is an integration bug. During the whitelist period (first 24 hours after deployment per the constructor), ALL calls to mintToken and mintWithBudget will revert with 'Not whitelisted user' because the on-chain leaf computation (20-byte address hash) will never match any leaf in the Merkle tree (42-byte string hash). The entire whitelist mint phase is non-functional. The owner can mitigate by either: (1) calling setRootHash with a correctly-generated root, or (2) calling setWhitelistEndTime to a past timestamp to bypass whitelisting entirely.
validation_result.impact_assessment: All whitelisted minting is blocked during the whitelist window. This is a launch-blocking DoS for the first 24 hours (per constructor: whitelistEndTime = block.timestamp + 24 hours). Users who were promised whitelist access cannot mint. Protocol revenue from the whitelist phase is lost. The owner can recover by updating the root hash or ending the whitelist early, but this damages trust and may cause community frustration.
recommended_mitigations: Fix genMerkleTreeLib.ts to hash addresses as 20-byte packed values: `const leaves = address.map(addr => keccak256(Buffer.from(addr.slice(2), 'hex')))` or use ethers.js `solidityPackedKeccak256(['address'], [addr])`
Add an end-to-end integration test that generates a Merkle proof in JS and verifies it passes the on-chain onlyWhitelisted modifier
Consider using OpenZeppelin's MerkleTree JS library which handles Solidity-compatible leaf encoding
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy contracts using deployTasks.ts. 2. Take a WHITELIST address, generate its proof using genMerkleTreeLib.ts. 3. Compute the on-chain leaf: keccak256(abi.encodePacked(whitelistAddress)). 4. Verify this leaf ≠ keccak256(addressString). 5. Call mintToken(proof) from the whitelist address during the whitelist period. 6. Observe the revert with 'Not whitelisted user'. 7. Alternatively, compute both hashes off-chain and show they differ.
uncertainties: The exact version of the `keccak256` npm package is not specified in the provided code. While the standard npm `keccak256` package treats string inputs as UTF-8, a non-standard implementation could theoretically handle hex-prefixed strings by converting to bytes. However, this would be highly unusual behavior.

The contents of the WHITELIST constant are not provided. If addresses were somehow stored as raw bytes rather than strings, the behavior would differ. However, TypeScript typing (`string[]`) and standard practice confirm they are strings.
additional_testing_needed: Verify the exact npm `keccak256` package version and its string-handling behavior
Write a Hardhat test that generates a Merkle proof using genMerkleTreeLib.ts and attempts to call mintToken on-chain during the whitelist period
Confirm WHITELIST addresses are standard hex-encoded address strings
